metadata.source_file: Rice recognition from Sentinel-1 SLC SAR data based on progressive feature screening and fusion.md
metadata.title: Enhancing rice recognition accuracy in SAR images through progressive feature fusion
metadata.abstract: a _College of Astronautics, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing 211106, China_ b _State Key Laboratory of Information Engineering in Surveying, Mapping and Remote Sensing, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China_ c _Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region Surveying and Mapping Achievement Center, Beijing 100830, China_
metadata.introduction:
Rice is a crucial staple feeding over half of the global population (Kuenzer and Knauer, 2013), utilizing more than 12 % of the world’s arable land (Nakai, 2018). Monitoring rice is vital for sustainable development (Orynbaikyzy et al., 2019). Optical remote sensing is pivotal in estimating crop area, monitoring growth, and predicting yields. However, regions where rice is cultivated often face challenges obtaining high-quality optical data due to persistent rainy and cloudy conditions (Liu et al., 2019; Zhu and Woodcock, 2012). Advancements in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) technology offer a promising solution. SAR is not hindered by weather conditions, allowing the acquisition of data in various seasons and weather scenarios, capturing dynamic 

surface changes. Rice recognition using SAR has gained attention (Mullissa et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019). Dualpolarized SAR, particularly sensitive to vegetation characteristics, proves valuable for accurate feature recognition, especially considering rice’s diverse scattering properties during different growth stages (Sivasankar et al., 2020). The Sentinel-1 satellite, equipped with four acquisition modes, including Strip Map (SM), Interferometric Wide Swath (IW), Extra Wide-Swath (EW), Wave (WV), enhances dualpolarization SAR data. Among these modes, IW is the primary choice for land applications. While Ground Range Detected (GRD) products lack phase information, limiting polarization analysis accuracy, SLC products offer more precise phase information, enhancing sensitivity to crop growth changes. This advantage aids in distinguishing crops from

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jag.2024.104196

> _S. Tian et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation 134 (2024) 104196_ 

land cover and enables finer surface characteristic analysis, essential for accurate rice identification. 

SAR image classification and recognition algorithms are typically categorized into statistical algorithms based on grayscale cell features (Xiang et al., 2013) and target decomposition algorithms based on electromagnetic wave scattering characteristics (Zhang et al., 2020). The pixel-based method analyzes target statistical characteristics and lacks a specific mechanism. Backscatter coefficient-based methods demonstrate varying patterns in rice growth cycles compared to other features (Inoue et al., 2014). Results indicate that rice’s backscatter coefficient changes with growth parameters (Yang et al., 2018). Utilizing HH and VV polarization, a precise rice identification is achieved by capturing the backscatter coefficient variation over time (Nguyen et al., 2015). However, characterizing rice growth cycle variations using the backscatter coefficient assumes a uniformly distributed canopy of tiny water droplets, which doesn’t hold true in field measurements. The uneven distribution of rice canopy layers horizontally, especially in the vegetative growth period, affects the main scattering mechanism due to changes in the interval between adjacent rice mounds. Polarimetric decomposition parameters also reflect rice’s electromagnetic wave scattering characteristics. The scattering characteristics obtained by H/α decomposition exhibit regular changes on the H/α classification plane (Izumi et al., 2017). H/α decomposition is a technique in SAR data analysis for interpreting radar echo scattering mechanisms. It distinguishes different surface targets and their scattering, crucial for surface classification and vegetation monitoring. Nevertheless, the presence of many scatterers in the zonal boundary region within the H/α classification plane poses challenges for their direct identification and classification using H/α decomposition parameters. Several studies highlight limitations of SAR image-based H/α decomposition as a monitoring basis for features during any growth period (Salma et al., 2022). The evolution of Sentinel-1 SLC products in rice recognition emphasizes comprehensive utilization of advanced features, data fusion, and advanced data analysis technology to enhance accuracy and broaden the application of rice recognition. 

Excellent classifiers are crucial for crops recognition, especially when dealing with multi-temporal polarization data (Wei et al., 2022). Statistical classifiers, such as Wishart (Liu et al., 2016), and standard machine learning algorithms like support vector machines (SVM), as well as deep learning networks such as recurrent neural networks (RNN), are employed to extract and interpret features. These approaches allow for a more comprehensive description of various feature attributes, thereby enhancing feature recognition capabilities (Ndikumana et al., 2018). In recent years, recurrent neural networks (RNNs), particularly long-term short-term memory RNNs (LSTM), have proven to be effective deep learning methods for end-to-end time series classification. LSTMs address the issue of gradient vanishing or explosion that may occur with increasing time series data. The application of RNNs to the time series classification of crops has gained traction. For instance, Bazzi et al. successfully used backscatter coefficient features in conjunction with recurrent neural networks to achieve a 97 % accuracy in classifying crops in France (Bazzi et al., 2019). 

This paper proposes a method to enhance rice recognition accuracy in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images by combining multi-temporal and multi-feature data. Instead of relying solely on the backscatter coα efficient, this approach integrates backscatter coefficient and H/ decomposition parameter features. The method automatically categorizes five ground features using features fusion. Firstly, using Gaussian fitting statistical distribution method, the coarse selection of samples with different features at different time is fuzzy extracted. Secondly, by analyzing the inter-group features of the samples, the time feature combinations of different time series features are established. Then, taking the features of the samples as input, the rice growth was expressed through the combination of the temporal growth height of rice and the radar scattering model. Thirdly, we use bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) network to express rice growth under SAR image sample 

features fusion. The accuracy of ground classification is evaluated before and after data fusion. Validation data is input into KNN (K-Nearest Neighbors), SVM (Support Vector Machine), and BiLSTM classifiers, demonstrating the feasibility of the data fusion method (DF: σ[f] ). The objective of this paper also compares single features (SF: VV, VH), combined features (CF:VV + VH;H*α; σ[f] ). And to enable us to identify generalizations of adjacent extended regions with sample features. 

Using the 2018 time-series Sentinel1 SLC data comprising the rice growth season from May to October, we investigate sample features that express the difference between rice growth and other land features, and apply progressive feature fusion to map rice in two neighboring cities in 2018. By combining the sample point screening under the fuzzy rough Gaussian fitting statistical distribution and the feature fusion model under the length and height curve of rice, a rice recognition method based on progressive feature screening and fusion was proposed, and the fused feature samples were applied to the BiLSTM recognition classifier. It has the potential to identify rice from two adjacent spliced Sentinel1 SLC images with a small number of samples. The progressive feature fusion method is very important for rice recognition, which will be helpful for resource management and food security.

evidence.original_text: Rice, a crucial global food crop, necessitates accurate mapping for food security assessment. China, a major rice producer and consumer, includes Jiangsu Province as a significant rice production region. The Hongzehu (HZH) area in Jiangsu contributes substantially to rice supply, supporting food security locally and province-wide.

Sentinel-1 SAR data, particularly Single Look Complex (SLC) products, holds promise for precise crop mapping with enhanced phase and polarization information, enhancing sensitivity to rice growth changes by analyzing rice surface features information.

To overcome this, our study proposes a progressive feature screening and fusion method using multi-temporal SAR images. We introduce fuzzy coarse screening based on statistical distribution characteristics and refine it with Gaussian fitting. A model incorporating time-series sample separation and polarization decomposition feature fusion based on rice growth height enhances rice growth expression. For more precise results, we advocate a multi-temporal feature fusion approach using optimized sample features in the BiLSTM network to characterize rice growth and ground features.

entries.term: progressive feature screening and fusion method
entries.normalized: 渐进式特征筛选与融合方法

evidence.original_text: Backscatter coefficient-based methods demonstrate varying patterns in rice growth cycles compared to other features (Inoue et al., 2014). Results indicate that rice’s backscatter coefficient changes with growth parameters (Yang et al., 2018). Utilizing HH and VV polarization, a precise rice identification is achieved by capturing the backscatter coefficient variation over time (Nguyen et al., 2015). However, characterizing rice growth cycle variations using the backscatter coefficient assumes a uniformly distributed canopy of tiny water droplets, which doesn’t hold true in field measurements.

H/α decomposition is a technique in SAR data analysis for interpreting radar echo scattering mechanisms. It distinguishes different surface targets and their scattering, crucial for surface classification and vegetation monitoring. Nevertheless, the presence of many scatterers in the zonal boundary region within the H/α classification plane poses challenges for their direct identification and classification using H/α decomposition parameters.

To enhance the LSTM, we use a bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) initialized according to time-series feature fusion. The idea of BiLSTM is to input the four extracted time series into two LSTMS in the forward and reverse directions respectively, then connect the implicit layers of the two networks together, and finally jointly access the output layer for prediction (Hameed et al., 2019). BiLSTM models, in contrast to oneway LSTM models, are more efficient in leveraging contextual information, as they consider both preceding and succeeding fragments when generating output at a specific time.

MIMICS (MIchigan MIcrowave Canopy Scattering) model (Liang and Pierce, 2004; Ulaby et al., 1990) is based on the microwave radiation transmission model, and the vegetation scatterers are decomposed into the canopy, trunk layer and ground surface layer of vegetation (Davitt et al., 2020; Mandal et al., 2020). The distribution of each organ of vegetation presents certain rules, which can be described through certain functional relationships. The corresponding microwave backscattering is divided into five parts.

In this study, polarization entropy H and mean scattering Angle α were used to characterize the changes of crop scattering information, and were combined with VV and VH backscattering coefficients to achieve data fusion. The characteristic fusion index ( σ[f] ) of polarization growth named DF method can be obtained.

To overcome this, our study proposes a progressive feature screening and fusion method using multi-temporal SAR images. We introduce fuzzy coarse screening based on statistical distribution characteristics and refine it with Gaussian fitting. A model incorporating time-series sample separation and polarization decomposition feature fusion based on rice growth height enhances rice growth expression. For more precise results, we advocate a multi-temporal feature fusion approach using optimized sample features in the BiLSTM network to characterize rice growth and ground features.

Sentinel-1 SAR data, particularly Single Look Complex (SLC) products, holds promise for precise crop mapping with enhanced phase and polarization information, enhancing sensitivity to rice growth changes by analyzing rice surface features information.

evidence.original_text: In this study, observations utilized the single look complex (SLC) Sentinel-1 (S1) product with a C-band dual polarization (VH + VV) mode. The S1 product offers a 12-day revisit period and all-weather imaging capabilities, with an incidence angle ranging from 32.189° to 34.21°. The data were acquired in Interferometric Wide Swath (IW) mode, featuring a width of 250 km and a resolution of 5 m * 20 m. The experimental Sentinel-1 SAR images were obtained from the Copernicus Open Access Hub. The study encompassed the entire growth cycle of rice in Suqian and Huaian cities. Throughout the growing season (mid-May to the end of October), 14 (7 * 2) Sentinel-1 images were composited from pairs captured at similar times to cover the study area comprehensively. The level-1 SLC products underwent processing steps, including orbit correction, radiation correction, deburst, polarization matrix, multi-looking, speckle removal, and geocoding. The study area’s data sets were categorized into five classes: rice, water, impervious surface, wood, and wasteland. The SAR dataset was constructed by merging high-resolution images from Google Earth and 10 m land cover classification maps (Karra et al., 2021) for sample selection. Using regions of interest with weakly supervised prior information can provide sample support for large-scale high-resolution land cover classification (Cui et al., 2024). To minimize errors in sample selection, impervious surface, wasteland, and water body samples were chosen based on attributes that remained consistent in the land cover – map over three years (2017 2019). Rice fields and wood samples were obtained in 2018 through sampling areas of interest identified in Google Earth images and field surveys. The sample set was then divided into training and test sets in an 8:2 ratio, with the former used for model training and the latter for verification. Detailed information on the sample set is provided in Table 1.

entries.name: Sentinel-1 SLC SAR time-series dataset for rice recognition

evidence.original_text: The radar signal determines the scattering characteristics of the target, and rice exhibits notable variations across different growth stages. In the training set, the backscattering coefficient for each category is computed. Unlike fully polarized scattering, dual-polarized SAR is characterized by a 2 * 2 covariance matrix (C2) (Mandal et al., 2019). C11 represents the VV backscattering coefficient, and C22 represents the VH backscattering coefficient. Notably, C21 and C22 are complex numbers, with the ’*’ symbol indicating complex conjugation. We employed the SNAP platform, developed by the European Space Agency (ESA), for the computation of C2. The polarimetric decomposition process, based on eigenvectors and eigenvalues, referred to as the H/α decomposition, was conducted using PolSARPro 6.0. In the context of the decomposition method, H and α are defined as follows: where (=1, 2) is the eigenvalue, and (=1, 2) is the corresponding eigenvector. H represents the entropy-based component and is a measure of randomness or disorder in the data. The value range of H is [0,1]. The value range of α is [0°, 90°], α stands for the alpha angle and is associated with the orientation or rotation of the scattering mechanism. It characterizes the polarimetric response of the target.

entries.description: Dual-polarized SAR data covariance matrix (C2) format for feature extraction

evidence.original_text: The sample set was then divided into training and test sets in an 8:2 ratio, with the former used for model training and the latter for verification.

entries.description: Sample set division protocol with training and test sets in an 8:2 ratio

evidence.original_text: In this section, we identified rice in two surrounding cities (Huaian City and Suqian City) of the study area, and compared the rice maps obtained by DF method combined BiLSTM (DF-BiLSTM) with rice products obtained by existing products of the study area globally and locally. The high-resolution products of 2018 single-season rice (10 m/ 20 m) named Shen rice map (Shen et al., 2023) in Suqian and Huaian, Jiangsu, China was used as comparison products. The method is based on time-weighted dynamic time by combining sentinel-2 and Sentinel-1 GRD data warping (TWDTW) method is used to recognize rice.

Under various fusion methods, a comparison of rice area recognition and statistical data fitting in the two cities, namely Suqian City and Huai’an, is illustrated in Fig. 16. In this paper, the different classification method (RF,SVM,BiLSTM) of two Cities was used to analyze the rice map under the fusion of features (DF), and the pixel method was used to calculate the DF methods to optimize and confirm the rice area obtained under the RF, SVM, BiLSTM methods. Compared with the agricultural statistical data, the rice area obtained by the DF-BiLSTM method had the best fitting effect with the agricultural statistical data. The DF-BilSTM method has the best fitting effect on rice area and agricultural statistics. Compared to the statistical data, the rice area in both municipalities is underestimated using the RF and BiLSTM classifiers.

entries.name: Agricultural statistical data for rice area validation

evidence.original_text: The proposed flowchart for time series SAR rice recognition, based on progressive feature screening and fusion, is depicted in Fig. 3. Initially, the Gaussian fitting model and JM separability method are jointly employed to screen sample characteristics during different periods. Subsequently, rice growth features are characterized by considering temporal feature fusion. During the H/α decomposition of dual-polarized Sentinel-1 SAR data, three parameters can be extracted: Alpha Angle (α), Entropy (H), and Anisotropy (A). In rice growth identification, typically only Alpha Angle (α) and Entropy (H) are considered, while Anisotropy (A) is not included. Alpha Angle (α) and Entropy (H) effectively characterize the scattering characteristics of rice. Representative and highly separable rice samples under temporal combination are further selected as additional training samples. These selected training samples are then combined with the MIMICS scattering model for feature fusion. The rice growth height under different features is fitted, and optimized feature fusion are obtained. The model is further trained by inputting the optimized weight features into BiLSTM. The optimized sample features are input into BiLSTM for additional model training. Finally, pixel-by-pixel image recognition is conducted, resulting in recognition maps for rice in two cities.

entries.name: progressive feature screening and fusion method

evidence.section: 2.3.1. Fuzzy coarse screening samples based on Gaussian fitting statistical distribution
evidence.original_text: Due to variations in the seeding time of rice across different plots on cultivated land, and the susceptibility of rice recognition on cultivated land to two types of surface features woods planted in cultivated land and newly opened wasteland, a fuzzy screening of different features for these three categories is necessary. This screening aims to select feature values within the fluctuation range of the statistical distribution model of frequency occurrence at a specific time, thereby expressing surface feature samples on cultivated land. Additionally, due to the distinct scattering characteristics of different ground objects, the backscattering coefficient and the polarization decomposition parameter Alpha, which vary with time, are crucial parameters in SAR ground object identification (Yang et al., 2021). To enhance the analysis of rice planting in neighboring areas and minimize errors stemming from planting time differences, a Gaussian fitting statistical distribution model was applied to the feature counts of three categories of different ground samples that were challenging to distinguish from rice on cultivated land (see Fig. 4). While mitigating errors caused by rice planting time in neighboring areas, points of interest representing various features of different samples were further selected.

entries.name: fuzzy coarse screening samples based on Gaussian fitting statistical distribution

evidence.section: 2.3.2. Feature fusion of model rice growth height based on enhanced MIMICS
evidence.original_text: To effectively utilize SAR image information, it is imperative to calculate the separation degree between different types of radar samples based on various combinations of time and phase. In this context, some sample points do not facilitate the differentiation of samples and make minimal contributions to sample separation; therefore, they should be excluded. The method used to determine the separability between samples is referred to as the J-M distance (Cai et al., 2019; Chang et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2021). The calculation formula of J-M distance as follows: where, p(x / wi) is the probability that the ith pixel belongs to category wi, and Jij is 0–2. The larger the value, the higher the degree of separation between samples. By comparing the size of Jij of different features with the coarse sample points, the four images of 5, 6, 8 and 10 months were finally selected for rice classification as the Table 2. At this time, the J-M distance of the three samples (wasteland, rice and wood land) was above 1.9, indicating a good sample separation degree. A reliable sample point representing the whole growth cycle can be obtained by combining the points of interest in each period.

entries.name: feature fusion of model rice growth height based on enhanced MIMICS

evidence.section: 2.3.3. The optimal sample after feature fusion on BiLSTM
evidence.original_text: Long short-term memory (LSTM), known for their exceptional time series learning capabilities, find extensive application in text recognition, time series prediction, natural language processing, computer vision, and various other domains (Thorp and Drajat, 2021). The LSTM unit controls the memory unit through the gating mechanism to effectively mine the growth characteristics of rice in the time dimension, thus completing the rice recognition task with high precision (Crisostomo de Castro Filho et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2022). To enhance the LSTM, we use a bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) initialized according to time-series feature fusion. The idea of BiLSTM is to input the four extracted time series into two LSTMS in the forward and reverse directions respectively, then connect the implicit layers of the two networks together, and finally jointly access the output layer for prediction (Hameed et al., 2019). BiLSTM models, in contrast to one-way LSTM models, are more efficient in leveraging contextual information, as they consider both preceding and succeeding fragments when generating output at a specific time (Crisostomo de Castro Filho et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2022). In order to fully understand the timing information of multiple features such as VH feature and single feature (SF) or combination feature (CF) or fusion feature (DF), a BiLSTM model was designed after the feature fusion of optimized sample selection.

In this study, observations utilized the single look complex (SLC) Sentinel-1 (S1) product with a C-band dual polarization (VH + VV) mode. The S1 product offers a 12-day revisit period and all-weather imaging capabilities, with an incidence angle ranging from 32.189° to 34.21°. The data were acquired in Interferometric Wide Swath (IW) mode, featuring a width of 250 km and a resolution of 5 m * 20 m. The experimental Sentinel-1 SAR images were obtained from the Copernicus Open Access Hub. The study encompassed the entire growth cycle of rice in Suqian and Huaian cities. Throughout the growing season (mid-May to the end of October), 14 (7 * 2) Sentinel-1 images were composited from pairs captured at similar times to cover the study area comprehensively. The level-1 SLC products underwent processing steps, including orbit correction, radiation correction, deburst, polarization matrix, multi-looking, speckle removal, and geocoding.

entries.task: rice recognition from Sentinel-1 SLC SAR data
entries.setup: Sentinel-1 SLC SAR images covering rice growth season (May to October) in Hongzehu area, Jiangsu Province, China

evidence.original_text: The study area’s data sets were categorized into five classes: rice, water, impervious surface, wood, and wasteland. The SAR dataset was constructed by merging high-resolution images from Google Earth and 10 m land cover classification maps (Karra et al., 2021) for sample selection. Using regions of interest with weakly supervised prior information can provide sample support for large-scale high-resolution land cover classification (Cui et al., 2024). To minimize errors in sample selection, impervious surface, wasteland, and water body samples were chosen based on attributes that remained consistent in the land cover – map over three years (2017 2019). evidence.section: 3.1.2. Comparison classification results of different features
evidence.original_text: The confusion matrix diagram of the BiLSTM classification based on test datasets is shown in Fig. 12. This depiction is based on diverse features and is conducted under the test set conditions, contributing to a comprehensive understanding of the classification performance. Table 4 lists five types of ground object accuracy indexes in BiLSTM based on different features in the predicted data set. Compared with other feature classification, the accuracy of rice with DF method is 94 %, which can better distinguish rice, wasteland and wood, and the overall accuracy is 93 %. Both all or single grounds recognition have high accuracy, which indicates the effectiveness of DF method.

entries.task: comparison of classification results using different features and classifiers
entries.setup: BiLSTM, SVM, RF classifiers tested with SF, CF, DF features

evidence.section: 3.3. Rice recognition expanded to two cities
evidence.original_text: In this section, we identified rice in two surrounding cities (Huaian City and Suqian City) of the study area, and compared the rice maps obtained by DF method combined BiLSTM (DF-BiLSTM) with rice products obtained by existing products of the study area globally and locally. The high-resolution products of 2018 single-season rice (10 m/ 20 m) named Shen rice map (Shen et al., 2023) in Suqian and Huaian, Jiangsu, China was used as comparison products. The method is based on time-weighted dynamic time by combining sentinel-2 and Sentinel-1 GRD data warping (TWDTW) method is used to recognize rice. As shown in Fig. 15. It can be seen that the overall range of spatial position of rice identified by comparison products and this study is consistent. The proposed method DF-BiLSTM has a good extraction effect on the boundary between rice and other ground objects.

entries.task: rice recognition expanded to two cities and comparison with existing products
entries.setup: DF-BiLSTM applied to Huaian City and Suqian City, compared with Shen rice map product

evidence.original_text: The progressive method significantly enhances accuracy in rice recognition from Sentinel-1 SLC images over time. The BiLSTM classifier with optimized samples fusion feature (DF) outperforms single features (SF: VV; VH) and combined features (CF: VV + VH; H * ɑ) in different classifiers. The overall classification map in the HZH study area achieves high accuracy with an OA and Kappa value exceeding 0.95 and 0.94, respectively. The rice maps of two cities are successfully generated by using the progressive feature screening and feature fusion DF method, which is suitable for the rice generation map of two cities spliced from two Sentinel-1 SLC images. The mapped rice area exhibits superior performance, with a fitted slope of 0.938 compared to statistical yearbook planting data. This research validates a viable rice recognition map based on time-series SAR, contributing to improved rice recognition in polarimetric SAR for widespread application in surrounding areas and cities. In the rice recognition study, the DF-BiLSTM classification demonstrated the highest performance, achieving an OA up to 95.22 % and a kappa value of up to 0.94. When compared to SVM and RF classifiers, the BiLSTM enhances the classification of various quantitative features. However, the DF method outperforms the SF and CF methods in terms of accuracy. Specifically, the overall classification accuracy of RF (91.28 % overall accuracy (OA)) under the DF method surpasses that of SVM (88.56 % OA). While the classification performance of BiLSTM surpasses that of RF, and RF outperforms SVM, it is worth noting that the differences in classification based on σ[f ] were not particularly significant.

entries.context: BiLSTM classifier with optimized samples fusion feature (DF) method for rice recognition in HZH study area

Compared with other feature classification, the accuracy of rice with DF method is 94 %, which can better distinguish rice, wasteland and wood, and the overall accuracy is 93 %. Both all or single grounds recognition have high accuracy, which indicates the effectiveness of DF method. The table shows 94.96 % in red font for highest rice recognition accuracy, 88.05 % in orange for second highest, and 95.22 % in red for overall classification accuracy. The confusion matrix diagram of the BiLSTM classification based on test datasets is shown in Fig. 12. This depiction is based on diverse features and is conducted under the test set conditions, contributing to a comprehensive understanding of the classification performance.

entries.context: DF method based on BiLSTM classifier for rice class

evidence.original_text: In the rice recognition study, the DF-BiLSTM classification demonstrated the highest performance, achieving an OA up to 95.22 % and a kappa value of up to 0.94. When compared to SVM and RF classifiers, the BiLSTM enhances the classification of various quantitative features. However, the DF method outperforms the SF and CF methods in terms of accuracy. Specifically, the overall classification accuracy of RF (91.28 % overall accuracy (OA)) under the DF method surpasses that of SVM (88.56 % OA). While the classification performance of BiLSTM surpasses that of RF, and RF outperforms SVM, it is worth noting that the differences in classification based on σ[f ] were not particularly significant. The progressive method significantly enhances accuracy in rice recognition from Sentinel-1 SLC images over time. The BiLSTM classifier with optimized samples fusion feature (DF) outperforms single features (SF: VV; VH) and combined features (CF: VV + VH; H * ɑ) in different classifiers. The overall classification map in the HZH study area achieves high accuracy with an OA and Kappa value exceeding 0.95 and 0.94, respectively. The rice maps of two cities are successfully generated by using the progressive feature screening and feature fusion DF method, which is suitable for the rice generation map of two cities spliced from two Sentinel-1 SLC images. The mapped rice area exhibits superior performance, with a fitted slope of 0.938 compared to statistical yearbook planting data. This research validates a viable rice recognition map based on time-series SAR, contributing to improved rice recognition in polarimetric SAR for widespread application in surrounding areas and cities. Under various fusion methods, a comparison of rice area recognition and statistical data fitting in the two cities, namely Suqian City and Huai’an, is illustrated in Fig. 16. In this paper, the different classification method (RF,SVM,BiLSTM) of two Cities was used to analyze the rice map under the fusion of features (DF), and the pixel method was used to calculate the DF methods to optimize and confirm the rice area obtained under the RF, SVM, BiLSTM methods. Compared with the agricultural statistical data, the rice area obtained by the DF-BiLSTM method had the best fitting effect with the agricultural statistical data. The DF-BilSTM method has the best fitting effect on rice area and agricultural statistics. Compared to the statistical data, the rice area in both municipalities is underestimated using the RF and BiLSTM classifiers. Additionally, plotting a 1:1 line from two points (i.e., cities) and fitting a trend line using the slope as an evaluation metric may only be appropriate for larger data sets or more complete plots. The slope fitting between DF-BiLSTM results and agricultural cohabitation data is 0.93, which is higher than that of other classification methods for rice products.

entries.metric: slope fitting with agricultural statistical data

evidence.original_text: Rice, a crucial global food crop, necessitates accurate mapping for food security assessment. China, a major rice producer and consumer, includes Jiangsu Province as a significant rice production region. The Hongzehu (HZH) area in Jiangsu contributes substantially to rice supply, supporting food security locally and province-wide. Sentinel-1 SAR data, particularly Single Look Complex (SLC) products, holds promise for precise crop mapping with enhanced phase and polarization information, enhancing sensitivity to rice growth changes by analyzing rice surface features information. However, challenges persist, especially climate impacts and timing inconsistencies between fields for planting rice. To overcome this, our study proposes a progressive feature screening and fusion method using multi-temporal SAR images. We introduce fuzzy coarse screening based on statistical distribution characteristics and refine it with Gaussian fitting. A model incorporating time-series sample separation and polarization decomposition feature fusion based on rice growth height enhances rice growth expression. For more precise results, we advocate a multi-temporal feature fusion approach using optimized sample features in the BiLSTM network to characterize rice growth and ground features. Experimental results demonstrate the method’s efficacy in two cities with a limited number of sampling points. The progressive feature fusion (DF) method outperforms classical classification methods using single feature (SF) or combined features (CF). Our proposed strategy proves effective for rice mapping applications, providing a promising approach for leveraging Sentinel-1 SLC SAR data.

This study proposes a method for improving rice recognition in Sentinel-1 SLC dual-polarized time-series SAR data based on progressive feature screening and fusion by combining the sample point screening under the fuzzy coarse Gaussian fitting statistical distribution with the feature fusion model under the rice growth height curve, the fusion feature samples are input to a BiLSTM network for rice recognition. The progressive method significantly enhances accuracy in rice recognition from Sentinel-1 SLC images over time. The BiLSTM classifier with optimized samples fusion feature (DF) outperforms single features (SF: VV; VH) and combined features (CF: VV + VH; H * ɑ) in different classifiers. The overall classification map in the HZH study area achieves high accuracy with an OA and Kappa value exceeding 0.95 and 0.94, respectively. The rice maps of two cities are successfully generated by using the progressive feature screening and feature fusion DF method, which is suitable for the rice generation map of two cities spliced from two Sentinel-1 SLC images. The mapped rice area exhibits superior performance, with a fitted slope of 0.938 compared to statistical yearbook planting data. This research validates a viable rice recognition map based on time-series SAR, contributing to improved rice recognition in polarimetric SAR for widespread application in surrounding areas and cities.

The data used in this paper is only Sentinel1 SLC data. Compared with the literature (Shen et al., 2023), Sentinel1 and Sentinel2 multisource data were used to identify rice as shown in Fig. 17 in a large-scale study area. The slope of the literature in two cities of Jiangsu Province in 2018 was 0.77, and the slope of the study area fitting in this paper was 0.94. It can be seen that the proposed method has a good effect on rice identification in the adjacent two cities. By adding the slope and fitting line as evaluation metric to the comparative experimental figure, the rice classification of the two cities in 2018 using the proposed method and the comparison literature method is determined. In the rice recognition study, the DF-BiLSTM classification demonstrated the highest performance, achieving an OA up to 95.22 % and a kappa value of up to 0.94. When compared to SVM and RF classifiers, the BiLSTM enhances the classification of various quantitative features. However, the DF method outperforms the SF and CF methods in terms of accuracy. Specifically, the overall classification accuracy of RF (91.28 % overall accuracy (OA)) under the DF method surpasses that of SVM (88.56 % OA). While the classification performance of BiLSTM surpasses that of RF, and RF outperforms SVM, it is worth noting that the differences in classification based on σ[f ] were not particularly significant. In conclusion, this method, combined with growth curve optimization and feature fusion, effectively leverages the phenological data of rice growth and establishes a connection with temporal SAR image information. This approach contributes to enhancing the accuracy of rice recognition. It’s worth noting that the weight information from the optimized feature fusion is subsequently integrated into the BiLSTM classifier. This enables the creation of a classification model for rice and other ground objects, facilitating pixel-by-pixel image prediction and successfully achieving rice recognition.

However, the proposed method still has some shortcomings, for example, seasonal water boundary, some paths between rice were wrongly classified as rice, and in the optimization model of expressing the special growth characteristics of rice, the expression of different characteristics is not balanced for different change categories.

Future developments in Sentinel-1 SLC products for rice identification should prioritize advanced features, data fusion, and advanced data analysis technologies to enhance accuracy and enable large-scale applications.
Another important future research direction is to balance the different feature expressions of different categories on time series images to identify rice performance in different growth cycles.
